meta.title: SF-SPSE-2: Identity, Data Protection, and Security Architecture

questions.stem: Which authentication method lets a service account connect to Snowflake without storing a password, using an RSA key pair instead?

options.explanation: Snowflake supports RSA key-pair authentication for programmatic access. **A** is federated SSO, **C** is not a primary auth method, and **D** is a network restriction, not authentication. [Key-pair authentication and key-pair rotation](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/key-pair-auth)

questions.stem: A company wants interactive users to authenticate through their enterprise identity provider. Which Snowflake capability is the standard fit?

options.explanation: SAML 2.0 federation is the standard Snowflake SSO pattern for enterprise IdPs. **A**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. [Overview of federated authentication and SSO](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-security-fed-auth-overview)

questions.stem: Which TWO controls are configured through Snowflake password or login policies? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Password policies can enforce complexity rules. **B** is correct because login policies can control failed login behavior. **C** and **D** are unrelated account settings. [User management](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-user-management)

questions.stem: A security engineer wants a non-person user that can call Snowflake from automation with no interactive password login. What is the most appropriate setup?

options.text: Create a human user with ACCOUNTADMIN
Use key-pair authentication for the service account
options.explanation: Service access should use a dedicated principal with a supported non-password authentication mechanism such as key pairs. **A** violates least privilege, **C** is not authentication, and **D** still lacks identity proof. [Key-pair authentication and key-pair rotation](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/key-pair-auth)

options.text: Use a network policy without any credential mechanism

questions.stem: What is the main benefit of federated SSO for enterprise security teams?

options.text: It removes the need for RBAC
It centralizes authentication with the identity provider
options.explanation: SSO centralizes authentication and lifecycle control in the IdP. **A**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect because RBAC and network controls still matter. [Overview of federated authentication and SSO](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-security-fed-auth-overview)

questions.stem: Which Snowflake principle is most directly implemented by building custom roles for job functions and granting only needed privileges?

options.explanation: Role design around job functions implements least privilege. **A**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated platform features. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: Which system role is typically used to manage grants, roles, and users in Snowflake?

options.explanation: SECURITYADMIN is the standard role for security administration in an account. **A** focuses on object management, **C** is not an admin role, and **D** is organization-scoped. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about Snowflake role hierarchies are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Privileges flow from child roles to parent roles that contain them
options.explanation: Parent roles inherit from granted child roles. **C** is correct because responsibility-based role design supports least privilege. **B** is unsafe, and **D** is false because inherited roles can convey privileges including ownership-derived capabilities. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

options.text: Granting every user ACCOUNTADMIN is a best practice for troubleshooting
Custom roles should usually be designed around responsibilities
OWNERSHIP can never be inherited through a granted role

questions.stem: Why is `MANAGE GRANTS` considered sensitive from a security perspective?

options.text: It enables centralized privilege administration across objects
options.explanation: `MANAGE GRANTS` allows broad grant administration, so it should be limited carefully. **A**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

options.text: It encrypts data with customer keys

questions.stem: Which Snowflake feature masks sensitive column values at query time based on policy logic?

options.explanation: Dynamic data masking applies policy-based masking to column results. **A** filters rows, **C** manages credits, and **D** controls session behavior. [Understanding Dynamic Data Masking](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-ddm-intro)

questions.stem: A company must show only each tenant’s own rows from a shared table. Which feature is the best fit?

options.explanation: Row access policies filter visible rows based on context. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not implement row-level data isolation. [Understanding row access policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-row-intro)

questions.stem: Which TWO statements correctly distinguish masking policies from row access policies? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Masking policies alter returned column values. **B** is correct because row access policies govern row visibility. **C** and **D** confuse data protection with compute control or core authorization. [Understanding Dynamic Data Masking](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-ddm-intro)

options.text: Row access policies decide which rows are visible
Masking policies can suspend a warehouse
Row access policies replace RBAC completely

questions.stem: What is the main security value of automatic sensitive data classification and tags in Snowflake?

options.text: They eliminate the need for users
They help discover and govern sensitive data consistently at scale
options.explanation: Classification and tags support scalable discovery, labeling, and downstream policy application. **A**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect. [Use SQL to set up sensitive data classification](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/classify-auto)

questions.reference: [Use SQL to set up sensitive data classification](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/classify-auto)

questions.stem: Which feature adds customer-managed key participation to Snowflake encryption while Snowflake still maintains its own keys?

options.explanation: Tri-Secret Secure incorporates a customer-managed key with Snowflake’s encryption model. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. [Tri-Secret Secure in Snowflake](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-encryption-tss)

questions.stem: What does a network policy restrict in Snowflake?

options.explanation: Network policies restrict authentication by client IP. **A**, **C**, and **D** are different controls. [Controlling network traffic with network policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: Why might a company adopt PrivateLink or Private Endpoint for Snowflake connectivity?

options.text: To keep traffic off the public internet
options.explanation: Private connectivity reduces exposure to the public internet. **B**, **C**, and **D** are not the purpose of the feature. [AWS PrivateLink and Snowflake | Snowflake Documentation](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-security-privatelink)

options.text: To replace MFA with private DNS
To create new custom roles automatically
To speed up Time Travel recovery

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about Snowflake network policies are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: They can be assigned at account or user level
options.explanation: Policies can be assigned broadly or per user. **B** is correct because they implement IP filtering. **C** and **D** confuse network controls with data protection and RBAC. [Controlling network traffic with network policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

options.text: They can enforce IP allow/block logic
They grant access to schemas when the IP matches

questions.stem: A contractor needs tighter login restrictions than employees, without changing the account-wide policy for everyone else. What is the cleanest approach?

options.text: Assign a user-level network policy to the contractor
options.explanation: User-level network policies support targeted restrictions for a specific identity. **B** is unsafe, **C** weakens security, and **D** is unrelated. [Controlling network traffic with network policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

options.text: Grant ACCOUNTADMIN so the contractor can self-manage access

questions.stem: Which view should you query first to review failed logins and MFA-related connection details?

options.explanation: `LOGIN_HISTORY` records authentication attempts and connection metadata. **A** is for data access, and **C** and **D** are usage/storage views. [LOGIN_HISTORY view](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/login_history)

questions.stem: Which view is best for tracing which objects a query read or modified during a security investigation?

options.explanation: `ACCESS_HISTORY` provides object-level access auditing for queries. **A** is less detailed for object lineage, **C** is role metadata, and **D** is configuration, not evidence of access. [ACCESS_HISTORY view](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/access_history)

questions.stem: Which TWO practices improve Snowflake compliance posture? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Regular audit review supports governance. **C** is correct because consistent tagging and policy application improve control coverage. **B** and **D** weaken compliance. [ACCESS_HISTORY view](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/access_history)

options.text: Apply consistent tags and governance policies to sensitive data
Ignore login telemetry if SSO is enabled

questions.stem: A security team needs to prove whether a masked column was accessed by a user yesterday. What is the most relevant evidence source first?

options.explanation: Access history provides the audit trail for object reads, which can be combined with masking policy context. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not show data access events. [ACCESS_HISTORY view](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/access_history)

questions.stem: Which design best supports separation of duties in Snowflake?

options.text: One shared admin role for everyone

options.explanation: Separation of duties means splitting object administration and security administration where appropriate. **A**, **C**, and **D** violate that principle. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

options.text: Giving analysts ownership of security policies

questions.stem: A global enterprise must align Snowflake access with HR identity lifecycle events such as joiner, mover, and leaver processes. Which approach fits best?

options.text: Manual local passwords for each user
Federated identity integration with role assignment governance
options.explanation: Federated identity integration centralizes lifecycle control and works well with governed role assignment. **A**, **C**, and **D** are weak security designs. [Overview of federated authentication and SSO](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-security-fed-auth-overview)

options.text: Only network policies without identity provider integration

questions.stem: Which TWO elements are core parts of a layered Snowflake security architecture? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Identity and RBAC are foundational. **B** is correct because data protection policies add defense in depth. **C** removes visibility, and **D** is not a security architecture principle. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

options.text: Data protection policies such as masking or row access

questions.stem: Why is broad use of ACCOUNTADMIN discouraged even in mature environments?

options.text: It concentrates too much privilege and complicates auditability
options.explanation: Concentrated privilege increases blast radius and weakens separation of duties. **A**, **C**, and **D** are false. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: A masking policy reveals full values only to approved roles, while all other roles see partially obscured output. What security principle does this illustrate?

options.text: Least privilege and role-based data exposure
options.explanation: Masking uses role-aware policy logic to expose only what is necessary. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. [Understanding Dynamic Data Masking](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-ddm-intro)

questions.stem: What is a key difference between network policies and external network access rules?

options.text: Network policies govern inbound login IP restrictions, while external network access rules govern outbound connections from supported code
options.explanation: Network policies protect inbound authentication and external network access rules control outbound connectivity from supported workloads. **B**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect. [External network access overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/external-network-access/external-network-access-overview)

options.text: They are identical features with different names
External network access rules manage warehouse sizing
Network policies are only for Tableau integrations

questions.stem: Which authentication setup is commonly used for browser-based workforce SSO into Snowflake?

options.text: SAML 2.0 with the enterprise identity provider
options.explanation: SAML federation is the common browser SSO approach. **B** is encryption-related, **C** is compute-related, and **D** is not the standard human SSO pattern. [Overview of federated authentication and SSO](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-security-fed-auth-overview)

options.text: OAuth client credentials for every human user in the browser

questions.stem: Which privilege model concept explains why object creators can accidentally create grant sprawl in regular schemas?

options.text: Object owners can grant privileges on what they own
options.explanation: Object ownership includes grant authority in regular schemas. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: Which feature is best for protecting sensitive values like national IDs while still allowing broad table access patterns?

options.explanation: Dynamic data masking protects column values without necessarily removing row access. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not protect data values directly. [Understanding Dynamic Data Masking](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-ddm-intro)

questions.stem: A security engineer wants to correlate a suspicious login with later object access. Which two history areas are most useful together?

options.explanation: Login telemetry and object access telemetry together support incident reconstruction. The other pairs do not provide the same audit trail. [LOGIN_HISTORY view](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/login_history)

questions.stem: An enterprise wants customer-managed key participation, federated identity, private connectivity, and data masking. What does this combination represent?

options.explanation: Combining identity, network, encryption, and data protection controls creates layered security. **B**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect. [Tri-Secret Secure in Snowflake](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-encryption-tss)

questions.stem: Which control should be used to limit allowed source IP ranges for all users by default?

options.explanation: An account-level network policy establishes the default IP-based login boundary. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. [Controlling network traffic with network policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about OAuth in Snowflake are generally correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: OAuth can support token-based access patterns for applications
options.explanation: OAuth enables token-based application access. **C** is correct because service-to-service patterns can use OAuth flows. **B** is false because RBAC still applies, and **D** is false because network controls remain useful. [Introduction to OAuth](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/oauth-intro)

options.text: OAuth replaces all Snowflake roles and privileges
OAuth can be relevant for service-to-service scenarios

questions.stem: Which role is usually preferred for routine object creation and management rather than account-wide security administration?

options.explanation: SYSADMIN is intended for object administration. **B** focuses on security administration, **C** is organization-scoped, and **D** is not an admin role. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: What is the main difference in outcome between a row access policy and a masking policy when a query runs?

options.text: Row access policies hide rows; masking policies alter visible values
options.explanation: Row access governs row visibility while masking changes returned column values. **B**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect. [Understanding row access policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-row-intro)

options.text: Masking policies hide rows; row access policies resize warehouses
They always produce the same result

questions.stem: Which kind of Snowflake evidence is most helpful for showing whether MFA was involved in a login event?

options.explanation: Login history contains authentication-related connection details. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. [LOGIN_HISTORY view](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/login_history)

questions.stem: Which TWO design choices improve least-privilege posture? (Select TWO)

options.text: Use job-function roles instead of one shared super-role
options.explanation: Job-function roles narrow privileges. **B** is correct because sensitive capabilities should be tightly controlled. **C** and **D** weaken governance. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

options.text: Limit sensitive grant capabilities to a small admin set
Grant ACCOUNTADMIN to all developers for emergencies
Use PUBLIC to own governed objects

questions.stem: A security engineer wants Snowflake-hosted code to call an external API safely. Which feature area should they study in addition to classic network policies?

options.text: External network access integrations and rules
options.explanation: Outbound calls from supported code rely on external network access features, not just inbound login policies. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not solve outbound connectivity governance. [External network access overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/external-network-access/external-network-access-overview)
